THE GREEN-COVERED MONTHLY.
By STEPHEN GWYNN, M.P.

Ireland is so small a province of the world, that even in the greater
Ireland, which has mo sea boundaries or land frontier, all workers are
familiarly knit together by a common recognition of each other’s exist-
ence. A friend 1s tnere, or an opponent, whom you may have never
mebt; but at least you are aware of him, you are linked by a fellow-
feeling, by no means limited to those of one purpose or party. He
may be wrong headed, he may be a pestilent nuisance; but after all
he is a part of Ireland.

This intimacy of attitude may be intelligible only to those who
share tne life of a small nationality ; but even Anglo-Saxons and the
other uninitiate (God nelp them) can conjecture how this tie of fellow-
ship heightens the peculiar friendliness inspired by a sympathetic per-
sonality diffusing itself over anonymous print. Such a friendly person-
ality I find in the directing genius of the friendliest publication thav I
know—one wholly concerned with the interests of Ireland, and desti-
tute of a single aversion. It would be cold praise to say that the
“Irish Book Lover ”’ is no mespecter of persons; it respects any person,
provided that he or she is Irish, and any hook, so long as it has to
do with Treland, At first séght this little green-covered monthly (but
‘1909 it appear with any mechanical swbservience to the calendar P—I
doubt it) geems no hetter than a scrap-heap of motes and queries, old
oxcerpts from some uncompiled catalogue, shreds of dry-as-dust erudi-
tion, flun g confusedly anto one bundle; yet the whole thing lives; ut

tadiates personality. When I come on it among a mass of circulars,

pamphlets, and otner such dead printed matber, it 18 like the sudden
greeting of o friend. Even 1ts advertisements have this vital quality ;
they differ from other advertisemonts as a good bookseller’s shop dif-
ferg from other shops; they are a cnallengo to conversation, an invita-
tion to loiter rather than to bo brief. I wonder af they can possibly bo

I have recently acquired a work entitled ‘ The Traveller in the
East: Being a Guide, etc., etc. By Godfrey Levinge, M.R.I.A., Lon-
don: Printed by the Autnor, Curzon Street, Mayfair, 1839.” (9 x 5} L
pp. X. + 331.) It is dedicated to Elizabeth Anne Lady Levinge, and \
hears an autograph inscription as follows: ‘‘For Captain George C. R. {

Levinge, from his affecte. Cousin, The Traveller, the Author and
Printer.”” An introduction covering three pages is dated ** London,
June, 1839, but the preface—to me the most interesting of the book
—bears date ‘‘ Cuilean, Mullingar, July, 1846.”” This preface occupies
two pages, and runs partly as follows:

PRINTING IN STRABANLE, 1801-1815.

The following list contains all the items of printing I have heep
able to note in the above town during the first quarter of the Dine.
teenth century. I have already published in pamphlet form the Prior
printing there. So this continues the list, and a comparison of the
two will make it more interesting, and an opinion can bhe formeq
whether printing in fhe town was progressing or mot. It has beap

pointed out to me, and I think very justly, that till the coming of |

railways our smaller towns had to, and did, depend more on thep.
selves for their printing and literature, as well as for other things, and

though the Union gave a very serious blow to our printing snd pub- |

lishing it was as regards the smaller towns the railways that gave a
final blow to this industry. The larger towns, and later England and
Scotland, supplied often the printing and literature necessary or de.
sired. I hope additions to this list may be supplied by our readers.

IRISH LITERARY SOCIETY.

On Sth January Dr. Crone presided at a crowded Tea Table Talk,
